item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh only the Foundation would tear a kids soul from a version of heaven FOR SCIENCE! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect the "2014" has on me is probably mitigated since it's, you know, 2019, but this skip is still pretty good. What I like the most are the footnotes where it states what she says as she does things. It adds a personal flair to the anomaly and makes me wanna hug it. +1

item.title: Down the rockerfeller street
item.content:encoded:
It's one of the few hidden gems around the wiki here, in my opinion
1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complains are that the circumstances of discovery of the skip, and therefore the backstory of the girl, could be simpler stated, and more complete. Since the Foundation found out the object short after Abigail's death, it shouldn't be phrased like it was hard to find about Abi and link her to the object and her specter.

And I think the reference to other skips on her room sounded forced by being unnecessary and not further developed. Either make something more about those, preferably with a short description or an expansion of this skip, or cut altogether this reference. The light bulb seems enough.

Otherwise, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this scip is well written and enjoyable, I must confess it feels unfinished. There's no real closure to it - she's just left there, and while that's understandable - it's what the foundation does - there isn't any real information regarding her. Does the need food? If the corpse is removed, is she removed? Why don't they try to put her back in for ease of containment? And so on. Neutral vote because the rest of it's great, but it does just feel unfinished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes me sad, and I love scips that make me sad. The magic nightlight is a fairly overdone trope, but you did it well so it doesn't matter. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That dialogue was more wooden than [insert famous actor here], and the overall storyness way too sappy and clichè for me to take seriously in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The feels…

The feels…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is both scary and rather tragic.

Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is scary as hell - mostly due to the fact that they just… left her there… which just proves that a SCP doesn't need to threaten the entire world to be profoundly disturbing.

+1, however, to everyone who said the dates should be redacted. They add nothing to the story, will become very silly for a brief period in the year 2014 (assuming we haven't experienced an xK event in the meantime), and then they will go back to adding nothing to the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WOW. I really love this scip. The fact she dies (or rather, "died") in 2014 is interesting, and it adds flavor to the object. The fact 1273-A finds her prison amazing and hates being drawn out is cool, and I like the interviews. Though the random sections of the interviewers notes really drew me out of the scip, it's still worth on upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… uh

Candy?
Actually the idea is to bait her with a help-offer, if she gives more information. I thought i said that.
As far as i can see, she wants to go back in the light and we want information about said light. We could tell her, that our scientists are working on the repair of the light and need her help.

If this would be a mainstream-TV series, we would ask her for any strange feelings, while in the light, go all CSI over the half episode and figure it out after the second try. (Which is "so damn obvious" and we should have thought of earlier)

As we are here at the SCP, trickery, lies and similar should do just fine in that part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, I mean how do you plan on forcing an incorporeal girl to do anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if you want to go back, you have to tell us the truth. Then we might find a way

Ask the junkie where the dealer is, with the excuse to get the drugs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: now we can force her to tell us the process. That would help both us and the girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it when the Foundation makes things horrifically worse by experimenting and thinking they know best. No heaven for you little girl, not until we finish analyzing its anomolous properties and figuring out a containment protocol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice one!

Yeah, you'd think they'd eventually try to change the lightbulb. But I like it left unexplained. It would be just terrible if they changed the bulb and it was still broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As everyone else has said, I think putting the date in the future shatters our willing suspension of disbelief, and our immersion. It's great otherwise, but I wonder, is it the NIGHTLIGHT that was anomalous, or the bulb? I'd really like to know.
If it was the nightlight, replace the damn bulb and put her back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…. How many D-class personnel does it take to change a lightbulb?

Aside from that minor omission, I really enjoyed this one. Throwing an upvote onto the pile.

On a side note, there are still a couple of confusing appearances of "subject" in the Mu-1 log (where footnote 4 appears and in the subsequent sentence).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got my snark removed when I visited the Snark Punishment Clinic.
Aah, fair enough. I'm not going to change it for this once, but I probably won't set it past the current date again, if only to avoid this hubbub.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awww, shucks!
Isn't there some rule about not being a big sweetie in the forums? A minimum snark and bile requirement? :D

I don't mind specific dates, per se. In fact, I think it makes very little in-universe sense to redact dates in most cases. It's the "from the future" aspect that jars, and my reaction isn't much changed by the time spans involved. Part of it is that I like to conceive of the Foundation existing in more or less our world, that they are keeping all these secrets right now as I write this. Documentation concretely from the future kind of shatters that, or else requires additional suspension of disbelief gymnastics about time travel. So I guess you could call it a matter of violating head-canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I care wishun
I always care <3
  • To me personally, it makes sense that these documents would at least have some dating of events. And it's really not that far into the future, I really don't know why so many people have a problem with this ._. It's just a thing
  • Acknowledged and changed
  • Auch, fair point
  • Fixed
  • Since it's clear I'm never ever going to get that imagery across clinically, I've finally given up and completely removed that word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lots of very minor things that irk, mostly matters of personal taste, but the good story trumps them. +1

In case anyone cares:
No one cares. You must have clicked accidentally.

-I don't like things that unambiguously date the Foundation, particularly those putting it in the future.
-" …turned on and connected to a working power source."
Is out of order, surely it makes more sense to plug it in first.
-"After this time, SCP-1273's anomalous effects will activate."
At any particular point after this time? Exactly at the 30 minute mark, or could it be any random time greater than 30 minutes?
-"…unknown ethnicity."
Why can't they tell? Race is pretty easy to tell from appearances, and it should be even easier once they found the real Abigail.
-"The entity appears to be opaque and is visually indistinguishable from a non-anomalous human subject…"
There are redundant redundancies here. If it is visually indistinguishable, that means (among other things) that it isn't translucent. Second, opaque/translucent is a matter of appearance, it goes without saying. There is no way to appear opaque while actualy being translucent. That would be an interesting visual paradox, though.
-"marionette" is poor tone. Good image, but not even close to clinical language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The prisons were 1) staying in the empty cell, or 2) staying in the corpse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, yes, it broke, but after it broke she said something like "don't make me decided between two prisons", so my impression was that at some point (perhaps always) she considered whatever was on the other side to be a prison, in spite of earlier saying that she like it there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personal backstory/possible Fifthism that might just be me misinterpreting the group '~'
Please ignore if it hinders your enjoyment of the article

It was a mostly implied hint towards possible Fifthism. Whether or not she died willingly is kind of irrelevant; the place she went to was purely inhabited by souls. The nightlight happened to drag her out of it and into the physical world, albeit without an actual physical form. She can force the soul out of another body by touching it just because that's what happens, and can use that body as a host, kind of. Except she doesn't want to, because physical bodies are prisons, and this world is a prison, and this is her Hell. The movements are so jerky because a) she is kinda unwilling to actually use the body, and b) she's not used to the burden of a physical form.

I dunno, that's just the personal story I made to go behind the skip. I think it works even without it and might not even be Fifthist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm your only friendI'm not your only friendBut really I'm not actually your friendBut I am

I'm looking, and I'm actually not sure what makes this Fifthist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The nightlight itself was a portal more than anything. And it broke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So when she said she enjoyed being in the nightlight, was she lying? Or did something about the inside or the nightlight change?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This whole thing made me feel bad and that's good, so +1 to this. Plus one to this indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Désolé

Of course, you can judge me all I want but each article must be judged separately. Regardless of children used in other articles.

But yeah I'll probably cut down on that now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm judging you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't forget that deleted one about the weird apartment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I chuckled

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it were shaped like a canary, then you could make a little soul house in your bird. (I'm sorry. I'll leave now.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…
looks at SCP-1517
looks at SCP-1357
It's not what it seems, I swear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You really like scips involving children and panicky interview logs don't you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A machine? No, I'm simply a writer that ran into an excess of inspiration within the last month.

I would change it, but, you see, even with the borderline tone, the point didn't get quite across. The control she has over the body isn't lifelike; rather, it's something trying to move the body in a way that can be useful to it. It doesn't know/remember how to move the body correctly. It's jerky, rough movements that the body is making.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What browser are you using that sticks ads in pages that don't have them? I recommend Firefox and Adblock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the first part, I would argue that the dialogue plausibly fits. Not all 16 year old girls are completely and utterly informal and devoid of respect and sense. She had just been forcibly removed from her quite literal comfort zone and placed in a stark room full of adults in lab coats and such. She's being respectful and fairly reserved because she's intimidated by the whole scene.

As for the second part, I suppose it's more of a stylistic choice than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should've been a streetlamp, but a rabbit-shaped nightlight is good too. This was a good read. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Azzle, you're a machine.

resulting in jerky movements similar to that of a manipulated marionette and

You don't need this. If you describe it as having "flung" itself at the door, we can assume that it doesn't have lifelike control without needing you to break tone to convey it.

Speaking of breaking tone; I find it a little odd to be reading through an article and at the end encounter a google ads advertisement. It's especially odd that the text and look of the add fits in with the text of the site itself, so I literally think that this article is asking me to

Join Leading Explorers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started out not great, but I liked the direction it went in, so I ultimately Upvoted. A couple of small notes though:
First, the girl's dialogue does not sound like spoken English, especially that of a modern (post modern?) 16 year old girl. It's too formal. Maybe it could use some modification.
Second, and I've seen this before… Why would the excerpts taken from the researchers notes be taken out mid sentence? This is not a burned piece of paper they recovered, they chose this sentence to put in an official log for containment purposes; they can include the beginning of a sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Acknowledged and changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems quite good. Story went in good directions, and I like how the nightlight dimension was actually nice to be in. One nitpick:

The object has been designed to resemble a stylized eastern cottontail rabbit (Sylvilagus floridanus),

Most nightlights I've seen don't have nearly enough detail to pick out an individual species of rabbit. There's an iconic rabbit look that they pretty much all go for. Moreover, the scientific name is utterly irrelevant. This seems to be clincalness for the sake of clinicalness. It doesn't make sense to include this rather than say "a nightlight in the shape of a rabbit".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really like the concept behind this.
The only thing that doesn't sit well with me is that it's set in 2014. I'm not a fan of "exposed" dates in SCP articles. Other than that, I don't really have anything else to add.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're trying to keep it contained. From what they've seen, the entity is able to kill and control any human (or possibly) living being that comes in contact with it, as well as being able to actually interact with things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I find most interesting is the implications of the Foundation leaving 1273-A effectively locked in that room, not even being opened for tests. Are they, erm, scared of what it would do? Or, maybe, just maybe, do they feel genuinely bad about what they've done and can't face it due to sheer shame?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This turned out to be more surprising than I anticipated. First, because she wanted to go back into the nightlight, and second because of that incident log. So they're just leaving this ghost girl in the containment area then, hm? A bit terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: gotdamit assel

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, not quite. You nixed the wrong "the subject".

Subject [SCP-1273-A] violently approached Agent ███████ and attempted to assault her while shouting at the subject [Agent ███████].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: feexed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject violently approached Agent ███████ and attempted to assault her while shouting at the subject4.
4 Subject was noted to say "This is your fault" repeatedly

This made me confused. The word "subject" appears to refer to one person at the beginning of the sentence, and another at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that imagery's just fucking disturbing, ain't it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
move about erratically in a way that resembled a marionette

This really, really bothers me. I understand what you're trying to say, but it seems to break tone to me.

However, I can emphasize with the skip here. +1 because I need tissues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"was reported dead as of 03/09/2014"

For some reason, that made me upvote.

Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2014

It's a skip from the future! RUN!
